questions.text: What does MPI stand for in the context of parallel computing?
questions.explanation: MPI stands for Message Passing Interface, a standard for communication in parallel computing.

questions.text: MPI is primarily designed for which type of computer architecture?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions MPI in the context of distributed memory machines and communication between different machines.

questions.text: In an MPI environment, what is the unique identifier assigned to each process?
questions.explanation: Addressing in MPI is handled by giving every process a unique identifier called a rank.

questions.text: If you start \( n \) processes using MPI, what is the range of the rank values?
questions.explanation: The rank value is always between 0 and \( n - 1 \).

questions.text: When was the first 'MPI: A Message-Passing Standard' introduced?
questions.explanation: The MPI Forum introduced the first standard in 1994.

questions.text: Which utility is typically used to start the required number of MPI processes?
questions.explanation: According to the text, 'mpirun' starts the required number of processes.

questions.text: What is the name of the default process group that includes all processes started at the beginning?
questions.explanation: All processes started by mpirun are organized in a process group called MPI_COMM_WORLD.

questions.text: Which of the following is true about `MPI_COMM_WORLD`?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is dynamic; processes can be added later.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of processes can change during execution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is static; the number of processes cannot change.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Participating processes can be swapped out for others.

questions.explanation: The text states that MPI_COMM_WORLD is static, meaning the number of processes and participating processes cannot change.

questions.text: How is a process uniquely identified in an environment with multiple groups?

choices.label: C
choices.text: By a tuple of (rank, process group)

questions.explanation: The rank of a process is always related to a specific process group; thus, (rank, process group) uniquely identifies it.

questions.text: If `MPI_Recv` is called and the specified sender rank exists but never actually sends a message, what is the result?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The MPI library returns an error immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The function returns a null buffer.

questions.explanation: If the rank exists but doesn't send, MPI_Recv waits forever, leading to a deadlock.

questions.text: What happens if a process attempts to receive a message using a tag that is outside the allowed range (e.g., greater than `MPI_TAG_UB`)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The MPI library can recognize it and return an error.

questions.explanation: If the tag is outside the allowed range, the MPI library can detect this and return an error.

questions.text: How many MPI functions are considered sufficient to program a distributed memory machine?
questions.explanation: The text mentions that six MPI functions are sufficient for programming a distributed memory machine.

questions.text: Which of the following is categorized as a 'Performance' reason to use advanced MPI functions?
questions.explanation: Collective operations, one-sided operations, and parallel I/O are listed under Performance.

questions.text: Which of the following is categorized as a 'Usability' feature in MPI?
questions.explanation: Process grouping functions, error handling, and language bindings are listed under Usability.

questions.text: In which year was MPI-2.0 completed?
questions.explanation: The history section states that MPI-2.0 was completed in 1997.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between MPI and standard protocols like TCP/IP as described in the text?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MPI is only for the Internet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: MPI defines interfaces/routines but does not specify a specific protocol.

choices.label: C
choices.text: TCP/IP uses ranks while MPI uses hostnames.

choices.label: D
choices.text: MPI requires every process to be started manually one by one.

questions.explanation: The text specifies that MPI defines interfaces/routines to send/receive data but 'does not specify a protocol'.

questions.text: If a process is part of multiple groups, which statement is true about its rank?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has the same rank in all groups.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It can only have a rank in MPI_COMM_WORLD.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has a specific rank within each group it belongs to.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ranks are only unique across the whole Internet.

questions.explanation: The text says 'a process has in each group a rank', implying it can have different ranks in different groups.

questions.text: What is a 'Tag mismatch' deadlock scenario in MPI?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The tag in `MPI_Recv` does not match the tag in `MPI_Send`.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tag is a negative number.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The process group does not support tags.

questions.explanation: If the tag specified in `MPI_Recv` differs from the one in `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv` waits forever, causing a deadlock.

questions.text: MPI-3.1 was finalized in 2015. What was its main purpose?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Restricting MPI to shared memory only.

questions.explanation: The text states that MPI-3.1 added small fixes to the previous version (3.0).

questions.text: Why would a programmer use 'one-sided operations' in MPI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To improve usability and error handling.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To simplify management of parallel applications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To fully exploit hardware capabilities for performance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because they are part of the basic six functions.

questions.explanation: One-sided operations are listed under the 'Performance' section as a way to exploit hardware capabilities.

questions.text: In `MPI_COMM_WORLD`, how many processes can have a rank of 0?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multiple if they are on different machines

choices.label: D
choices.text: Depends on the number of nodes

questions.explanation: The text states that every process has a unique identifier (rank) and no identifiers are duplicate.

questions.text: What occurs if an MPI library detects that a destination rank in a send operation does not exist?

choices.label: C
choices.text: It sends the message to rank 0 instead.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It creates a new process for that rank.

questions.explanation: The text states: 'if rank does not exist... the MPI library can recognize it and return an error'.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT listed as a reason to go beyond the basic six MPI functions?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing the number of allowed machines on the Internet

questions.explanation: Performance and Usability (including abstracting patterns) are explicitly listed as reasons. Increasing the number of machines on the Internet is not.

questions.text: Parallel I/O is considered which type of MPI feature?
questions.explanation: Parallel I/O is listed under the Performance sub-bullets.

questions.text: What defines the process startup mechanism in the 'Message Passing universe'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Manual startup of each application separately

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mechanisms provided by the MPI library to start \( n \) processes

questions.explanation: The text explains that while typical internet processes start separately, MPI provides mechanisms to start \( n \) processes working on the same problem.

questions.text: According to the history provided, which version of MPI was finalized in 2012?
questions.explanation: MPI-3.0 was finalized in 2012.

questions.text: In a group of 7 processes, what are the identifiers (ranks) of the processes?
questions.explanation: Ranks start at 0 and go to \( n - 1 \). For \( n=7 \), this is 0 to 6.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a 'sender mismatch' that leads to an error rather than a deadlock?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sender sends a message with the wrong tag.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sender rank specified does not exist in the communicator.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sender exists but is busy with another task.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sender and receiver are on the same machine.

questions.explanation: The text says if a rank does not exist, the library can recognize it and return an error. If it does exist but doesn't send, it deadlocks.

questions.text: Object attributes and language bindings are classified under which category?
questions.explanation: Both are listed as sub-bullets under the Usability section.

questions.text: What does the MPI Forum represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single company that owns MPI

choices.label: B
choices.text: A consensus-based effort to create a portable parallel computing standard

choices.label: C
choices.text: A government agency regulating internet traffic

questions.explanation: The text describes the MPI Forum as a consensus-based effort to create a portable parallel computing standard.
